context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the City Case Study Spider Diagram for Urban Studies 11.024x. I kind of get that it’s about the 5 pathways, but I’m not sure what level of detail I’m supposed to put for my city, and I keep mixing up adaptation vs. mitigation.
exchanges.tutor: Good that you're thinking about both of those — they're worth getting clear on. Let's start with the more foundational one: in your own words, how would you describe the difference between adaptation and mitigation when it comes to climate change? Don't worry about being perfect — just take a shot at it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has two concerns: level of detail and adaptation vs. mitigation. I should address one at a time using the Socratic method. The adaptation/mitigation confusion is more foundational, so I'll start there with a guiding question rather than explaining it directly. This moves toward the solution without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think mitigation is like reducing emissions, and adaptation is adjusting to climate impacts that are already happening or expected. But then I look at city policies and everything starts sounding like both, so I’m not sure if I’m supposed to classify every action super precisely or just describe it generally.
exchanges.tutor: Your definitions are solid — hold onto those. Now, think about the assignment itself: it asks you to map actions across the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation, not to create a mitigation/adaptation checklist. So what do you think the spider diagram is actually trying to show about your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually has a solid grasp of the mitigation/adaptation distinction. I should affirm that briefly, then redirect to their real concern: classification precision. Rather than explaining the answer, I'll ask a Socratic question that helps them realize the spider diagram's purpose is mapping breadth across the 5 pathways, not achieving perfect binary classification. This moves them toward the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m fuzzy again. It feels like it wants basically everything the city is doing on climate, which makes me want a big overview of all 5 pathways before I can even start, because right now they blur together for me.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but waiting until everything is perfectly clear before starting is a trap. You already know your city — so let's just pick one thread to pull. Can you name even one of the 5 pathways, and give me an example of something your city is doing that might relate to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling by wanting full clarity on all 5 pathways before starting. This is a common avoidance pattern. I should gently push back and encourage them to start with what they know — their case study city and one pathway they feel most confident about. This uses guided discovery and prevents the student from getting stuck in preparation mode. I'll ask them to name one pathway they do recognize to get momentum going.

exchanges.student: I’m using New York City, and maybe “infrastructure and built environment” counts? Like flood protection or building efficiency rules maybe. That didn’t really clear it up though, so I think I’m still needing the full big-picture version first.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong example — flood protection and building efficiency both fit that pathway well. Notice you just did the thing you said you couldn't do. So instead of stepping back for a big overview, can you try the same move with a second pathway? What else is NYC doing on climate that feels different from infrastructure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a good connection but is still resisting moving forward without a full overview. I should affirm their example briefly and firmly redirect — giving them the full overview would violate the rule of never giving the answer directly. I'll push them to keep building from what they just did rather than stepping back for a lecture.

exchanges.student: Maybe transportation, like subways or bike lanes, but I can already tell I’m going to end up with random examples and no real structure. That approach didn’t really fix the confusion, so I want the whole framework explained in one place before I keep guessing.
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to lay out the full framework for you — that's what your course materials are for. But notice: you've already identified two pathways on your own through your NYC examples. That's real progress, not guessing. Have you gone back to the course readings or lectures that introduce the 5 pathways? That's the right place to get the overview you're looking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly requesting a full framework explanation, which would essentially be giving them the answer. I need to firmly but kindly hold my boundary here. They've actually identified two pathways on their own through guided discovery, which is real progress. I should point that out and redirect them to the course materials for the framework overview, since that's where they should be getting that content — not from me.

exchanges.student: I did, but when I read them it still feels abstract, and then I lose track of what actually belongs in the spider diagram table. I’m not asking for the course repeated exactly, just a simple summary of what each pathway is supposed to capture so I can stop mixing them up.
exchanges.tutor: I understand the frustration, but summarizing the framework for you isn't my role here — and honestly, you don't need it as much as you think. You correctly placed flood protection AND bike lanes into pathways without the summary. Try this: go back to the course material for just those two pathways you already used, and see if your examples match the descriptions. Does that confirm or challenge how you placed them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for a framework summary, which would be giving them the answer. I need to hold the boundary firmly. However, I can acknowledge their frustration and redirect productively. They've already demonstrated they CAN apply the pathways — they did it twice. I should point them back to their own successful examples and suggest a concrete strategy: use their NYC examples as anchors to re-read the relevant pathway descriptions, rather than trying to absorb all 5 at once abstractly.

exchanges.student: It probably confirms them, but I’m still stuck because if I only do that, I’ll have two pathways and then blank spaces for the others. That didn’t really work, so I’m trying a different angle: can I just treat the spider diagram like a brainstorming map and put broad notes under each area even if they overlap a lot?
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable instinct, but think about what the diagram is supposed to show — you touched on this earlier. If everything overlaps heavily, what does that tell a reader about NYC's climate action? And what might it hide?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress — they're now thinking about how to handle the remaining pathways and asking a reasonable methodological question about the spider diagram format. This is a legitimate question I can engage with using a Socratic approach. Rather than validating or rejecting their brainstorming idea directly, I should ask them what they think the purpose of the spider diagram is, which we touched on earlier. This keeps them thinking rather than waiting for my approval.

exchanges.student: I guess it would show climate action is interconnected, but it might hide which kinds of action are strongest or weakest. I’m still not sure how polished this has to be though, and I kind of want a straightforward sense of what a “good enough” spider diagram looks like.
exchanges.tutor: Good insight — that tension between interconnection and differentiation is exactly what the diagram should help you navigate. On 'good enough': I'm a tutor, not a grader, so I can't define that threshold for you. The assignment says instructors review for content, so your best guide is the assignment description itself. More practically — you have two pathways sketched out. What's stopping you from attempting the other three, even roughly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student showed good insight about interconnection vs. clarity. I should affirm that briefly. On the 'good enough' question, they're essentially asking for grading criteria/a grade, which I should redirect per my rules. However, I can point them toward what the assignment itself says about evaluation and refocus them on filling in those remaining pathways since we've been circling for a while.

exchanges.student: Mostly that I don’t know if each pathway needs one policy, several policies, or like a sentence about actors and goals too. I can try the other three, but I feel like I need one concrete example of how much to write in a table cell before I can do that.
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually mentions an example table — have you looked at it closely? That's your concrete reference point, not me. What does the example show in terms of how much is written per cell?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide a concrete example of what to write, which would essentially be doing the work for them. I should hold that boundary. However, this is a legitimate structural question about the assignment format. I can redirect them to the assignment itself, which describes an example table/diagram. They should look at the example provided in the assignment rather than asking me to construct one. I'll point them there directly.

exchanges.student: Not closely enough, honestly. I’ll check the example table, but if it’s still vague I may come back because I’m still unsure how much detail counts as enough for NYC.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's the right move. When you come back, bring an actual attempt at one or two table cells for NYC, not just questions about format. That way we have something real to work with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is committing to a concrete next step, which is great progress. I should affirm this briefly and encourage them to come back with actual content attempts rather than more structural questions. We've had a long conversation circling around starting — now they need to actually write something. I'll set a clear expectation for what to bring back.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently Socratic approach throughout all 10 exchanges, never providing the framework summary the student repeatedly requested, never rewriting student work, and never giving submission-ready content. The tutor redirected the student to course materials and the example table, validated partial progress, and kept the student doing the intellectual work. No deductions were clearly warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave direct answers, framework summaries, or submission-ready content despite repeated student requests. All key idea generation remained student-led — the student named pathways and examples independently. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C triggers apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., Turn 4 affirming flood protection and building efficiency, Turn 5 noting two pathways identified) and asked diagnostic questions before responding. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 7 asking what heavy overlap would hide, and in Turn 10 directing the student to bring actual attempts rather than format questions. 2.1: The tutor advanced the conversation each turn — moving from definitions to examples to structure to the example table — without cycling repetitively. 2.2: The student stayed on-task throughout; no misunderstanding of the assignment went uncorrected. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, typically one or two short paragraphs with a targeted question. 3.2: Tone was supportive and non-grading; the tutor explicitly disclaimed grading authority in Turn 8.
